claims.evidenceExcerpt: We've discussed why sandboxes are important in both research and product. Uh but they have slightly different needs.
claims.text: The speaker argues that sandbox clouds have to serve different workloads in research and product, with research optimizing for throughput and product optimizing for latency, while both demand reliability and security.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: Um if you've seen the previous diagrams, uh there's the the fundamental problem with containers is that they're still native processes uh running on the host.
claims.text: He argues that containers improve isolation and resource control, but they still run native processes on the host and therefore leave the host kernel reachable.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: Uh so, turns out Linux provides a very very nice thing called virtualization, uh which is hardware powered at the CPU level.
claims.text: He argues that hardware virtualization gives a stronger security boundary because a compromised guest can still be contained from the host at the CPU level.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: Like so, if your harness wants to explore multiple like solutions or sample spaces, it can actually checkpoint the sandbox state and it can like do a Monte Carlo like tree search and like go ahead and like backtrack, checkpoint again.
claims.text: He argues that checkpointing sandbox state enables backtracking search and long-running exploration, including Monte Carlo tree search style rollouts over many days.

claims.evidenceExcerpt: And so, here is a way where we can use snapshot rich restore for better orchestration. So, remember we discussed that a snapshot can have a lineage of many, many layers.
claims.text: He argues that orchestration can use snapshot lineage to route restores to nodes that already have the needed layers, reducing download work and speeding recovery.

methods.description: Separate sandbox design around runtime isolation, persistence, and orchestration rather than treating execution as a single problem.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: There are many many parts of a sandbox cloud, but we'll specifically focus on runtime. So, how can we run uh sandbox on one node securely?
methods.name: Three-pillar sandbox design

methods.description: Reduce kernel attack surface with syscall filtering and argument constraints, while accepting that unknown workloads can break the filter.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: Uh one example to do this is something called seccomp. So, you can actually have a filter on the amount of system calls uh your container can call, and you can also control the arguments each system call can take.
methods.name: Seccomp filtering

methods.description: Move untrusted execution behind a hardware virtualization boundary so guest compromise does not directly imply host compromise.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: Uh so, turns out Linux provides a very very nice thing called virtualization, uh which is hardware powered at the CPU level.
methods.name: Hardware-isolated guest execution

methods.description: Store only changed disk extents between versions so snapshot and restore stay cheap enough for frequent use.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: So, Linux has these XFS file systems uh XFS-like file system. So, you can do pretty pretty much have zero latency copies because you don't change any blocks when you copy, but when you actually change the blocks on a file, that's when you pay the penalty.
methods.name: Incremental copy-on-write snapshots

methods.description: Route restores toward nodes that minimize snapshot-layer download cost.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: And so, here is a way where we can use snapshot rich restore for better orchestration. So, remember we discussed that a snapshot can have a lineage of many, many layers.
methods.name: Snapshot-aware placement

methods.description: Use a warm pool together with memory snapshots to cut startup latency without keeping too many idle resources hot.
methods.evidenceExcerpt: And so, here like we can use some micro VM features to support low latency creation ideas.
methods.name: Hybrid warm-start strategy

questions.evidenceExcerpt: Um, and then a lot of sandboxes these days have GPU access, presumably for auto research or some sort of like ML research type of agents.
questions.question: What is the best way to support GPU access in micro VMs without giving up the security boundary the speaker prefers?
questions.whyItMatters: GPU access is important for some agent workloads, but the talk treats it as one of the hardest gaps in the micro VM approach.

questions.evidenceExcerpt: But it doesn't happen till we have really, really rock solid primitives to do this. And so, given the needs and the the the pillars we want to support, here are some of the things that this snapshotting solution should support, First at ChatGPT or Codex scale, we want to do incremental snapshotting and what that means is like if you call snapshot twice, I'm just snapshotting the diff between the two snapshots.
questions.question: How can snapshotting stay incremental, cheap, and fast enough when sandbox state grows to gigabytes and snapshots are taken frequently?
questions.whyItMatters: The speaker presents low-cost incremental snapshotting as necessary for scale and for keeping the user experience responsive.

questions.evidenceExcerpt: And I think our models are just very good at anything POSIX compliant and standard. So, you can write a you can write a file system actually on top of a GCS or S3 or durable block storage.
questions.question: What storage architecture best delivers POSIX-like behavior and durable persistence on top of object storage without hurting sandbox performance?
questions.whyItMatters: The talk argues that persistence is central, but also notes that the backing storage and filesystem abstraction are still design-sensitive.

questions.evidenceExcerpt: And so, here like we can use some micro VM features to support low latency creation ideas.
questions.question: When should a platform use a warm pool, a memory snapshot, or a hybrid of the two for sandbox startup?
questions.whyItMatters: Startup strategy directly affects latency, idle resource cost, and fleet efficiency.

payload.summary: The talk argues that agent sandboxes should be treated as a cloud primitive built to run untrusted code securely, reliably, and at scale, not just as a convenience wrapper around local execution. It compares fork/exec, containers, gVisor, and micro VMs, and concludes that hardware virtualization is the cleanest security boundary even though it introduces performance and device-sharing tradeoffs. The second half argues that durable disk persistence is the next major unlock for agents, because incremental snapshotting, fast restore, and snapshot-aware scheduling let long-running tasks survive failures, support backtracking search, and scale across a fleet.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: There are many many parts of a sandbox cloud, but we'll specifically focus on runtime. So, how can we run uh sandbox on one node securely?
takeaways.text: Sandbox clouds need to balance runtime isolation, persistence, and orchestration because research cares most about throughput while product cares most about latency, and both need reliability and security.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: So, yeah. The this is the fight like to sum it up like containers interact with the same host kernel, so they do they do have some protections, but at the end it's the same host kernel they're trying to attack, right?
takeaways.text: Containers help, but they still share the host kernel, so they are only a partial answer to hostile or buggy agent code.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: We'll see how that works, but that is a key trade-off here. There's a performance penalty you pay every time the CPU is switching back and forth between these two modes.
takeaways.text: Hardware virtualization strengthens the security boundary, but the speaker is explicit that the gain comes with a performance penalty from guest-host context switching.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: Yeah, so that's the persistence part of the presentation. And so, the one takeaway I want you guys to think about is I think storage is the next unlock here.
takeaways.text: Disk persistence is presented as the next unlock for agent sandboxes because it turns short-lived computers into durable workspaces that can recover from failures.

takeaways.evidenceExcerpt: And so, here is a way where we can use snapshot rich restore for better orchestration. So, remember we discussed that a snapshot can have a lineage of many, many layers.
takeaways.text: Orchestration can become snapshot-aware, using lineage and node state to place restores where they are cheapest and fastest.

tools.description: User-space kernel-based sandboxing layer discussed as an intermediate isolation option.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: Uh can we do better? Can we reduce the host kernel exposure uh a little bit more? Yeah, so the second thing we can discuss is uh to solve the attack surface problem is something called Gvisor.
tools.name: gVisor

tools.description: Virtual machine monitor used as the classic Linux virtualization path in the speaker's comparison.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: It The VMM stands for virtual machine monitor. This software is called a virtual machine monitor, and you might have heard of QEMU when you search search for Linux virtualization.
tools.name: QEMU

tools.description: Rust-based VMM presented as an early example of the newer micro VM approach.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: So, the first the first Rust-based VMM was something called CrosVM, which our team at Google wrote when I was there to support Linux virtual machines on top of Chromebooks.
tools.name: CrosVM

tools.description: Rust-based VMM cited as a micro VM implementation used in serverless contexts.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: Cross VM was the first like Rust based VMM that came before and then Firecracker forked Cross VM and it's used on Amazon for their Lambda and serverless load.
tools.name: Firecracker

tools.description: General-purpose VMM named as part of the newer micro VM ecosystem.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: And Cloud Hypervisor is a more general like VMM that many many companies like contribute to and when you historically see micro VMs on the internet like it's powered by one of these VMMs basically.
tools.name: Cloud Hypervisor

tools.description: Paravirtualized device interface used for efficient guest-host communication.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: And so, they talk via something called virtio, uh which is a more efficient way of the guest and the host talking to each other.
tools.name: virtio

tools.description: Linux hypervisor API used by VMMs to launch and manage guests.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: QEMU and other VMMs, their sole job is to talk to dev KVM. So, you see that arrow going down like the dev KVM is the hypervisor API of the Linux kernel.
tools.name: KVM

tools.description: Guest-host communication channel used between the sandbox and the host.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: Uh, in this case we are using Vsock, which is a socket that can help you communicate between the guest and the host.
tools.name: Vsock

tools.description: Object storage mentioned as a backing layer for persistent sandbox storage.
tools.evidenceExcerpt: And I think our models are just very good at anything POSIX compliant and standard. So, you can write a you can write a file system actually on top of a GCS or S3 or durable block storage.
tools.name: S3

topics.description: How to execute agent tool calls and untrusted code securely for product and research workloads.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: So, a sandbox is a play an environment in which you can run these tool calls and execute code on behalf of the model securely and it could be on your laptop or it could be on the cloud.
topics.name: Agent sandboxing

topics.description: Using CPU-level virtualization to isolate hostile guest code from the host kernel.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: Uh so, turns out Linux provides a very very nice thing called virtualization, uh which is hardware powered at the CPU level.
topics.name: Hardware virtualization

topics.description: Saving sandbox state so long-running tasks can resume after failure or backtrack across branches.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: And let's go into like three big use cases on what persistence can unlock, right? So, counterintuitively, persistence actually helps reliability and scale.
topics.name: Disk persistence

topics.description: Using load, snapshot lineage, and restore cost to place sandboxes efficiently across a fleet.
topics.evidenceExcerpt: And so, here is a way where we can use snapshot rich restore for better orchestration. So, remember we discussed that a snapshot can have a lineage of many, many layers.
topics.name: Snapshot-aware orchestration

talkTitle: 'From fork() to Fleet: Designing an Agent Sandbox Cloud Pt2'
